purpose: Phase 6 Epstein Timeline + Cross-Reference Map. This creates a chronological case board so readers can move from date to person/entity, evidence class, source door, what the record supports, and open questions.
boundary: A timeline is a navigation tool, not a verdict. It shows sequence, record type, source door, people/entities, and evidence class. It must not merge association, allegation, settlement, conviction, and speculation into one category.

items.title: Florida plea and conviction baseline
items.recordSupports: Epstein's convicted-offender status and the legal baseline for later post-conviction access questions.
items.openQuestions: Who continued contact after conviction, who helped reputation repair, and what institutions treated the conviction as disqualifying or not?

items.title: Email/contact contradiction lane later becomes visible
items.recordSupports: Later-released/reported email material creates a timeline lane for presence, contact, denials, redactions, and contradiction analysis.
items.openQuestions: Which exact emails are authenticated, which names were redacted, and what is supported by the wording rather than commentary?

items.title: Giuffre v. Maxwell civil-record lane
items.recordSupports: A major court-record door into allegations, denials, deposition fights, unsealing disputes, and evidence references.
items.openQuestions: Which claims were sworn, which were denied, which were settled, and which were later corroborated or rejected by court action?

items.title: Political/media strategy email lane
items.recordSupports: Reported email threads support a media-leverage and narrative-management lane around Epstein-related questions.
items.openQuestions: What did each email say, who received it, and did any later statement conflict with the documented thread?

items.title: Federal Epstein prosecution and detention lane
items.recordSupports: Federal criminal process, indictment/prosecution materials, bail/detention arguments, and renewed public scrutiny.
items.openQuestions: Which documents entered the federal record, what was sealed or redacted, and what institutional failures became visible?

items.title: Epstein death in custody lane
items.recordSupports: Custody failure, death in federal detention, oversight questions, and continuing public-record controversy.
items.openQuestions: Which failures were documented, which theories remain unsupported, and which records would materially change the public understanding?

items.title: Maxwell arrest and prosecution lane
items.recordSupports: The start of Maxwell's criminal case, indictment/prosecution process, and evidence path into the later conviction.
items.openQuestions: Which evidence categories were used in court and which public claims remained outside the trial record?

items.title: Maxwell conviction lane
items.recordSupports: The strongest public-record lane against Maxwell: conviction record, trial evidence, and court process.
items.openQuestions: What did the jury decide, what counts survived appeal/litigation, and what claims were outside the conviction record?

items.title: Maxwell sentencing lane
items.recordSupports: The sentencing record and judicial consequence of the conviction.
items.openQuestions: Which victim statements, sentencing findings, appeal issues, and later challenges matter to the record?

items.title: Large civil-file unsealing lane
items.recordSupports: Unsealed records created new public search paths into names, exhibits, claims, denials, and association categories.
items.openQuestions: Which names were central, which were peripheral, which references were claims, and which documents changed evidence strength?

items.title: Reported Epstein email-release lane
items.recordSupports: Reported/released email material supports contact, narrative-management, knowledge-claim, and contradiction-lane questions.
items.openQuestions: Which emails are authenticated, which people are named, what exact wording is in the file, and what does each email prove or not prove?

items.title: House Oversight testimony and staff-logistics lane
items.recordSupports: Reported testimony and oversight activity support scheduling, staff knowledge, logistics, and cross-check questions.
items.openQuestions: Which staff records exist, what was denied, what was corroborated, and which testimony conflicts with survivor or FBI records?

items.title: Maxwell habeas and prosecutors' opposition lane
items.recordSupports: A new legal challenge and prosecutorial response create a live litigation lane around newly released materials and conviction-challenge arguments.
items.openQuestions: Which claims are procedural, which are evidentiary, which are rejected, and what does the court actually decide?

crossReferenceRules: Each timeline item must include a date, evidence class, people/entities, source door, what the record supports, and open questions.
Names in timeline entries are record nodes, not automatic guilt labels.
Repeated names across timeline, email, people, file cockpit, and network architecture sections become stronger network signals.
Court findings and convictions outrank reporting, email trails, contact records, and speculation.
Open questions must state what evidence would confirm, narrow, or falsify the theory.
